[Final Data Journalism .md](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29670130/Final.Data.Journalism.md)
### **Understanding The Populations Being Affected by Drug Overdose**

The data came from the CDC's State Unintentional Drug Overdose Reporting System, also known as SUDORS. SUDORS is known for collecting data on drug overdose deaths based on death certificates, medical and coroner reports, as well as postmortem toxicology results. The source is trustworthy as it is a government agency, so there are many regulations and restrictions that need to be met before they release any data to the public. We can ensure that they have reported data through a standardized method and meet some sort of integrity standard. Moreover, the CDC does maintain the data by integrating records from local and state jurisdictions. Since their data is taken from medical institutions and surveys, we can corroborate this data with medical facilities and hospitals directly. The CDC does have an agenda of protecting the health of the citizens and limiting disease. While this stands true, they have constant policy changes as the administration changes their standards. 

### **Methods**

The goals for this analysis were to understand the populations that are affected the most by drug overdose and the patterns we see as the years progress. For one of the pivot tables, I found the total number of deaths to occur per year. Each year from 2020 to 2023, the number of deaths seemed to be increasing. There was a spike in 2023, and then the deaths decreased in 2024\. I found that to be really interesting, after digging deeper–it seems that in 2023 the after-effects of the pandemic caused uncontrollable usage of fentanyl. In 2024, they were able to reduce the after-effects and limited the amount sold to the public because of the spike and chaos caused in 2023; this along with the effects of COVID-19 dying out.  

Another table I created was to compare the deaths males had to females. From past research and discussions I have had, I knew that males suffer mental health issues on a larger scale than females. Beyond that, with the increased toxic masculinity males are more prone to induldging in the drugs with the "masculinity tag" that drugs have. With this, I was expecting males to have a larger proportion of deaths from overdose than females. That served to be true, however, after taking a deeper look it also seems that physiologically, men are more vulnerable to the effects of fentanyl. 

The last table I made was a table to understand which states had the highest amount of drug overdose deaths. The highest deaths were shown to be in Pennsylvania and the lowest in Wyoming. Pennsylvania has lots of fentanyl infiltration and many animal tranquilizers (considered to be an illicit drug) are processed in the state, potentially correlated to higher chances of people using these drugs. However, it is important to note that within the dataset California is not mentioned, due to the very decentralized way of processing their state's data. 

[Link to Google Sheets](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1wF2BwxE2dxK8UBGD3d2_KDye1h-pAd2yA_azJE6OTQQ/edit?usp=sharing)

### **Ethical Concerns**

For this dataset there are a number of ethical concerns. For a long period of time there have been debates and concerns on the way the CDC takes their data. As the CDC takes a portion of their data from public health surveys, there is the concern of giving patients the autonomy on releasing their data. While this form of data collection was there to reduce exclusion of data, it has also sparked the concern of people’s data being collected without asking for explicit patient consent. There is also the concern that with overdose data, a lot of populations are not surveying disadvantaged populations as often as populations with more developed areas–possibly with better medical facilities. That being said, many populations are not included within the dataset and it can result in skewed statistics. In this dataset, a clear missing population was California, so addressing the lack of certain people's data is key to understand the true impact of drug overdose across the nation, as we are missing at least about 12 percent of the nations data. 

### **Addressing Limitations**
In order to ensure that the story is complete and ethically presented, I would try to understand the proportion of neglected marginalized communities with most of CDC’s datasets. Based on that, I would be able to leave a note on the analysis or reaggregate the data. As far as the data collection method for the CDC, I would not be able to control that. However, I could present the data after understanding which parts are important to mention about the patient and which are excessive and possibly invasive. With the limitation of not having all of the state's data, we could corroborate the missing data pieces from other datasets or medical institutions of regions that use a more decentralized way of gathering data.  

### **Citations**

Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. SUDORS Dashboard: Fatal Drug Overdose Data | Overdose Prevention U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, 28 May 2026, www.cdc.gov/overdose-prevention/data-research/facts-stats/sudors-dashboard-fatal-overdose-data.html.
